analogy: Think of "Rise of Nationalism in Europe" through the chapter's own opening images: 3 Nationalism in Europe In 1848, Frédéric Sorrieu, a French artist, prepared a series of four prints visualising his dream of a world made up of ‘democratic and social Republics’, as he called them. 1) of the series, shows the peoples of Europe and America... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Rise of Nationalism in Europe is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 3 Nationalism in Europe In 1848, Frédéric Sorrieu, a French artist, prepared a series of four prints visualising his dream of a world made up of ‘democratic and social Republics’, as he called them.

The text explains that 1) of the series, shows the peoples of Europe and America – men and women of all ages and social classes – marching in a long train, and offering homage to the statue of Liberty as they pass by it.

The text explains that as you would recall, artists of the time of the French Revolution personified Liberty as a female figure – here you can recognise the torch of Enlightenment she bears in one hand and the Charter of the Rights of Man in the other.

The text explains that on the earth in the foreground of the image lie the shattered remains of the symbols of absolutist institutions.

The text explains that in Sorrieu’s utopian vision, the peoples of the world are grouped as distinct nations, identified through their flags and national costume.

The text explains that leading the procession, way past the statue of Liberty, are the United States and Switzerland, which by this time were already nation-states.

The text explains that france, T h e R i s e o f N a t i o n a l i s m i n E u r o p e Fig.

The text explains that 1 — The Dream of Worldwide Democratic and Social Republics – The Pact Between Nations, a print prepared by Frédéric Sorrieu, 1848.

The text explains that chapter I The Rise of Nationalism in Europe New words Absolutist – Literally, a government or system of rule that has no restraints on the power exercised.

The text explains that in history, the term refers to a for m of monarchical government that was centralised, militarised and repressive Utopian – A vision of a society that is so ideal that it is unlikely to actually exist In what way do you think this print (Fig.

The text explains that india and the Contemporary World 4 identifiable by the revolutionary tricolour, has just reached the statue.

The text explains that she is followed by the peoples of Germany, bearing the black, red and gold flag.

The text explains that interestingly, at the time when Sorrieu created this image, the German peoples did not yet exist as a united nation – the flag they carry is an expression of liberal hopes in 1848 to unify the numerous German-speaking principalities into a nation-state under a democratic constitution.

The text explains that following the German peoples are the peoples of Austria, the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies, Lombardy, Poland, England, Ireland, Hungary and Russia.

The text explains that from the heavens above, Christ, saints and angels gaze upon the scene.

The text explains that they have been used by the artist to symbolise fraternity among the nations of the world.

The text explains that their existence is a guarantee of liberty, which would be lost if the world had only one law and only one master.’ Source Source A Summarise the attributes of a nation, as Renan understands them.

The text explains that discuss New words Plebiscite – A direct vote by which all the people of a region are asked to accept or reject a proposal

The text explains that a wave of economic nationalism strengthened the wider nationalist sentiments growing at the time.

The text explains that the German people have realised that a free economic system is the only means to engender national feeling.’ Source Source B Describe the political ends that List hopes to achieve through economic measures.

The text explains that is it not a disgrace that even the stupidest cattle-herder possesses the right to vote, simply because he is a man, whereas highly talented women owning considerable property are excluded from this right, even though they contribute so much to the maintenance of the state?’ Source Source C New words Feminist – Awareness of women’s rights and interests based on the belief of the social, economic and political equality of the genders

The text explains that the failure of revolutionary uprisings both in 1831 and 1848 meant that the mantle now fell on Sardinia-Piedmont under its ruler King Victor Emmanuel II to unify the Italian states through war.

The text explains that the most serious source of nationalist tension in Europe after 1871 was the area called the Balkans.

The text explains that how was the history of nationalism in Britain unlike the rest of Europe?

The text explains that why did nationalist tensions emerge in the Balkans?

The text explains that find out more about nationalist symbols in countries outside Europe.

The text explains that for one or two countries, collect examples of pictures, posters or music that are symbols of nationalism.

The text explains that how are these different from European examples?

The text explains that write a note on: a) Guiseppe Mazzini b) Count Camillo de Cavour c) The Greek war of independence d) Frankfurt parliament e) The role of women in nationalist struggles 2.

The text explains that what steps did the French revolutionaries take to create a sense of collective identity among the French people?

The text explains that what was the importance of the way in which they were portrayed?

The text explains that briefly trace the process of German unification.

The text explains that what changes did Napoleon introduce to make the administrative system more efficient in the territories ruled by him?

Source: NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II, Chapter 1: Rise of Nationalism in Europe

prelims_traps: Do not treat EVENTS AND PROCESSES and SECTION I as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Rise of Nationalism in Europe; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use EVENTS AND PROCESSES and SECTION I to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

options: This chapter will deal with many of the issues visualised by Sorrieu in Fig.
